text: Last class: I have managed this holding for ten years, and every target has been surpassed.
Today: under this agreement, each party shall deliver the goods, and the warranty must cover twelve months.

Either party may terminate the agreement with sixty days notice.

Confidential information must not be disclosed.
I have been through the agreement. Under clause four, each party shall deliver the goods within thirty days, and the counterparty shall act in good faith.
Good. And the requirements? The agreement stipulates that the warranty must cover twelve months, and that confidentiality is protected, the data must not be disclosed?
Exactly. And the permissions use may: either party may terminate with sixty days notice, and either party may request an audit once a year.
And what is the governing law, and the jurisdiction if there is a dispute?
The law of New York. And there is a force majeure clause, so if delivery is prevented, neither party is in default.
Then I am comfortable. The goods shall be delivered, the payment must be made on time, and the contract may be renewed. I hereby agree to the terms.
The goods shall be delivered exactly as the contract stipulates.

Maria Claudia, it is David. I have just been through the new Supply Agreement with our lawyer, so let me walk you through the key clauses before you sign. First, the obligations, and these use shall. Under clause four, each party shall act in good faith, and we, as the counterparty, shall deliver the goods within thirty days of every order. Second, the requirements, and these use must. The agreement stipulates that the warranty must cover twelve months, and that all confidential information must not be disclosed to any third party. Notice the passive there: the information must not be disclosed. Third, the permissions, and these use may. Either party may terminate the agreement with sixty days notice, and either party may request an audit once a year. There is also a force majeure clause: if delivery is prevented by events beyond our control, neither party shall be in default. The governing law is the law of New York, and any dispute shall be settled in the courts of that jurisdiction. One last thing, and you know this is the habit to watch: keep the passive for the obligations. Say the goods shall be delivered, the payment must be made, the contract may be renewed. Read it tonight and call me in the morning.

Right, let me make sure I understand this Supply Agreement before I sign it. David walked me through it, so let me say it back in my own words. There are three kinds of rule here. The obligations use shall: each party shall act in good faith, and we shall deliver the goods within thirty days. The requirements use must: the warranty must cover twelve months, and confidential information must not be disclosed. And the permissions use may: either party may terminate with sixty days notice, and either party may request an audit. The contract stipulates the governing law, which is the law of New York, and the jurisdiction for any dispute. There is a force majeure clause too, so if something beyond our control happens, neither party is in default, and we have a clear remedy. And the habit I always have to fix is the passive. I want to say the goods shall be delivered, not the goods shall deliver; the payment must be made, not the payment must make; confidential information must not be disclosed. So tomorrow, when I explain this to my team, I will be precise: shall for the obligations, must for the requirements, may for the permissions, and the passive for every term. Then I will sign.

First, the draft agreement arrives, and you read every clause carefully with your lawyer. Then you check the obligations: what each party shall do, and what must happen on time. Next, you check the permissions: what either party may do, and how the contract may be terminated. After that, you negotiate the points you disagree with, in good faith, until both sides accept the terms. Finally, the agreement is signed, the obligations take effect, and the goods shall be delivered exactly as the contract stipulates.
